news_summary: Today's technology news highlights breakthroughs in AI-driven protein research, advancements in battery technology enabling more practical energy storage, and a significant particle discovery at CERN resolving a longstanding scientific mystery.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans la recherche sur les protéines grâce à l'intelligence artificielle, des progrès dans le domaine des batteries permettant un stockage d'énergie plus pratique, ainsi qu'une découverte importante de particules au CERN qui résout un mystère scientifique de longue date.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan los avances en la investigación sobre proteínas impulsada por la inteligencia artificial, los progresos en la tecnología de las baterías que permiten un almacenamiento de energía más práctico y un importante descubrimiento de partículas en el CERN que resuelve un misterio científico de larga data.

articles.title: AlphaFold hits ‘next level’: the AI database now includes protein pairing
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 17 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00787-3The database of 200 million protein-structure predictions now includes homodimers, adding new biological relevance.
articles.content:
AlphaFold is now capable of predicting homodimeric complexes, including those formed by the transcription elongation factor Eaf, the N‑terminal region of which is shown here.Credit: Google DeepMind/EMBL-EBI (CC-BY-4.0)

A database containing the predicted structures of nearly every known protein on Earth has grown even larger and become more useful for understanding how the building blocks of life work together.

For the first time, the AlphaFold protein-structure database will include predictions of complexes of proteins — with the addition of 1.7 million ‘homodimers’ comprising two interacting strands of the same molecule.

The freely available database, maintained by the European Molecular Biology Laboratory’s European Bioinformatics Institute (EMBL-EBI) in Hinxton, UK, currently holds around 200 million predictions of individual protein structures, made using the AlphaFold2 artificial-intelligence tool, developed by London-based firm Google DeepMind.

Since its release in 2021, this repository has become a bedrock in discovery and a first port of call for research projects that try to understand life at the molecular level. But previous iterations of the database lacked predictions of how proteins form complexes, which can be indispensable for their function. For instance, HIV-1 protease — a viral protein that is a key drug target — works only when two copies of the same protein form a working enzyme.

AlphaFold is five years old — these charts show how it revolutionized science

Such proteins were already included in the database as individual ‘monomers’ but their entries tell only part of their story. “We thought, ‘can we bring the AlphaFold database to the next level, where we can include a lot of complex predictions across the tree of life?’” says Martin Steinegger, a computational biologist at Seoul National University in South Korea, who was part of the effort.

Complex interactions

To make predictions for even small complexes of two proteins was a crucial challenge, says Steinegger. “It is quite a different beast than monomer predictions.” Protein-complex predictions are exceedingly intensive computationally, so a consortium — including Steinegger’s laboratory, EMBL-EBI, Google DeepMind and chipmaker NVIDIA in Santa Clara, California — was formed to take on the challenge.

The consortium focused on protein complexes from 20 of the most studied species, including humans, mice, yeast and bacteria that cause disease in humans, such as Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
articles.reasoning: The expansion of the AlphaFold protein-structure database to include homodimeric protein complexes represents a major breakthrough in biological and medical research. This advancement enhances understanding of protein interactions crucial for life and disease, with broad implications for drug discovery and molecular biology. The database is freely accessible and impacts a wide range of species, benefiting the global scientific community.
articles.personality_title: AlphaFold expands to predict pairs of proteins, improving biological insights
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – AlphaFold is an artificial intelligence (AI) tool created by Google DeepMind that predicts the shapes of proteins. Since 2021, its database has held about 200 million predictions of individual protein structures from many living things. Proteins often work by joining together, but until now, AlphaFold mainly showed single proteins alone.

**What happened** – The AlphaFold database has added predictions for 1.7 million 'homodimers,' which are pairs of the same protein molecule joined together. This upgrade was done by a group of researchers from places like the European Molecular Biology Laboratory, Google DeepMind, and NVIDIA. They focused on proteins from 20 commonly studied species, including humans, mice, yeast, and bacteria that cause disease.

**Impact** – This is important because proteins often need to pair up to do their jobs, such as the HIV-1 protease enzyme that only works as a pair. Before, scientists could only see one half of these proteins, but now they can see how they connect and interact. This makes it easier to study how proteins work in the body and can help in discovering new medicines.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will use this new data to better understand protein interactions in health and disease. The team may expand the database further to include even more complex protein groupings. This will continue to support scientists worldwide in exploring biology at the molecular level.

**One-sentence takeaway** – AlphaFold’s new ability to predict protein pairs opens a clearer window into how proteins work together, aiding science and medicine.
articles.personality_title_fr: AlphaFold élargit ses prédictions aux paires de protéines pour mieux comprendre la biologie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – AlphaFold est un outil d’intelligence artificielle (IA) créé par Google DeepMind qui prédit la forme des protéines. Depuis 2021, sa base de données contient environ 200 millions de prédictions de structures de protéines individuelles provenant de nombreux êtres vivants. Les protéines fonctionnent souvent en s’assemblant, mais jusqu’à présent, AlphaFold montrait surtout les protéines seules.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La base de données AlphaFold a ajouté 1,7 million de prédictions de « homodimères », c’est-à-dire des paires de la même molécule de protéine assemblées. Cette amélioration a été réalisée par un groupe de chercheurs du Laboratoire européen de biologie moléculaire, de Google DeepMind et de NVIDIA. Ils se sont concentrés sur les protéines de 20 espèces très étudiées, dont l’humain, la souris, la levure et des bactéries pathogènes.

**Impact** – C’est important car les protéines doivent souvent s’associer pour fonctionner, comme la protéase du VIH-1 qui n’est active qu’en paire. Avant, les scientifiques ne voyaient qu’une moitié de ces protéines, maintenant ils peuvent observer comment elles se connectent et interagissent. Cela facilite l’étude du fonctionnement des protéines dans le corps et peut aider à découvrir de nouveaux médicaments.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs utiliseront ces nouvelles données pour mieux comprendre les interactions protéiques en santé et maladie. L’équipe pourrait élargir encore la base de données pour inclure des groupes de protéines plus complexes. Cela continuera à soutenir les scientifiques du monde entier dans l’exploration de la biologie moléculaire.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La nouvelle capacité d’AlphaFold à prédire les paires de protéines offre une meilleure compréhension de leur travail ensemble, aidant la science et la médecine.
articles.personality_title_es: AlphaFold amplía sus predicciones a pares de proteínas para mejorar la comprensión biológica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – AlphaFold es una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (IA) creada por Google DeepMind que predice la forma de las proteínas. Desde 2021, su base de datos contiene alrededor de 200 millones de predicciones de estructuras de proteínas individuales de muchos seres vivos. Las proteínas suelen funcionar uniéndose, pero hasta ahora AlphaFold mostraba principalmente proteínas solas.

**Qué pasó** – La base de datos de AlphaFold ha añadido 1,7 millones de predicciones de 'homodímeros', que son pares de la misma molécula de proteína unidas. Esta mejora fue realizada por un grupo de investigadores del Laboratorio Europeo de Biología Molecular, Google DeepMind y NVIDIA. Se centraron en proteínas de 20 especies muy estudiadas, incluyendo humanos, ratones, levaduras y bacterias que causan enfermedades.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque las proteínas a menudo deben unirse para funcionar, como la proteasa del VIH-1 que solo trabaja en pareja. Antes, los científicos solo veían una mitad de estas proteínas, pero ahora pueden ver cómo se conectan e interactúan. Esto facilita estudiar cómo funcionan las proteínas en el cuerpo y puede ayudar a descubrir nuevos medicamentos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores usarán estos nuevos datos para entender mejor las interacciones de proteínas en la salud y la enfermedad. El equipo podría ampliar aún más la base de datos para incluir agrupaciones de proteínas más complejas. Esto seguirá apoyando a científicos de todo el mundo en la exploración de la biología molecular.

**Resumen en una frase** – La nueva capacidad de AlphaFold para predecir pares de proteínas abre una ventana más clara sobre cómo trabajan juntas, ayudando a la ciencia y la medicina.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two intricately intertwined, ribbon-like protein strands glowing softly as they form a harmonious complex, set against a gentle, abstract background suggesting a molecular landscape, symbolizing the breakthrough in predicting protein pairings by AlphaFold’s AI database.

articles.title: Planar Li deposition and dissolution enable practical anode-free pouch cells
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 17 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10402-0Planar Li deposition and dissolution enable practical anode-free pouch cells
articles.content: Anode-free lithium metal batteries (AFLMBs), which are manufactured without anode active material, offer great potential for high-energy-density, low-cost energy storage. However, AFLMBs face a long-standing challenge of short lifespan due to the harsh conditions of lacking excess Li-resource and an anode host1-8. This issue is associated with uneven Li deposition/dissolution, rooted in the micro-heterogeneity and mechanical fragility of solid electrolyte interphase (SEI)9. Here we report a practical 500 Wh kg–1-level AFLMB with enhanced lifespan, achieved using a crossover-coupled electrolyte. The electrolyte triggers crossover-coupled interfacial reactions that generate a B–F-based polymer-rich SEI at the anode while suppressing gas evolution at the cathode. The resulting SEI exhibits sub-nanometer homogeneity, high flexibility, and rapid Li-ion transport, and it spontaneously develops a self-adaptive mesh-film structure that ensures uniform ion flux and large-volume-change accommodation, thereby realizing reversible planar Li deposition/dissolution of 5.6 mAh cm–2. Consequently, a 2.7 Ah AFLMB (508 Wh kg–1, 1668 Wh L–1) without any host-material coating demonstrates stable cycling for 100 cycles at 100% depth of discharge (DoD) and 250 cycles at 80% DoD, with 80% capacity retention and a high-power output of 2650 W kg–1 at 96 Wh kg–1. These findings establish crossover-coupled interphase chemistry and address the inherent structural instability of host-free electrodes, advancing the practical implementation of AFLMBs.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in lithium metal battery technology by achieving practical anode-free pouch cells with high energy density, enhanced lifespan, and stable cycling performance. This advancement addresses a major challenge in energy storage technology, potentially enabling more efficient, cost-effective batteries with broad applications in electronics and electric vehicles, thus having a meaningful positive impact on society and the environment.
articles.personality_title: New lithium battery design boosts energy and lifespan without an anode
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Lithium metal batteries are important for storing energy in devices and electric cars. A special type called anode-free lithium metal batteries (AFLMBs) can store more energy and cost less because they don’t use an anode material. But so far, these batteries have a short life because the lithium inside doesn’t deposit evenly, causing damage.

**What happened** – Scientists have developed a new battery that solves this problem by using a special electrolyte. This electrolyte creates a thin, flexible layer on the battery’s surface that helps lithium spread out evenly and protects the battery during charging and discharging. The team tested a battery with this design that could store a lot of energy and kept working well for hundreds of charge cycles.

**Impact** – This is important because it makes anode-free lithium batteries more practical for real use. The new design helps the battery last longer and work safely without extra parts, which can reduce costs and weight. This improvement could lead to better batteries for phones, laptops, and electric cars.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely work on making this battery design easier and cheaper to produce. They may also test it in different conditions and devices to confirm its durability and safety before it can be widely used.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new battery design using a special electrolyte layer enables longer-lasting, high-energy anode-free lithium batteries that could improve future electronics and electric vehicles.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle conception de batterie lithium améliore énergie et durée sans anode
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les batteries lithium métal sont essentielles pour stocker l'énergie dans les appareils et les voitures électriques. Un type spécial appelé batteries lithium métal sans anode (AFLMB) peut stocker plus d'énergie et coûter moins cher car il n'utilise pas de matériau d'anode. Mais jusqu'à présent, ces batteries ont une courte durée de vie car le lithium ne se dépose pas de manière uniforme, ce qui cause des dommages.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont développé une nouvelle batterie qui résout ce problème grâce à un électrolyte spécial. Cet électrolyte crée une couche fine et flexible à la surface de la batterie qui aide le lithium à se répartir uniformément et protège la batterie lors des charges et décharges. L'équipe a testé une batterie avec ce design capable de stocker beaucoup d'énergie et de fonctionner efficacement pendant des centaines de cycles.

**Impact** – C'est important car cela rend les batteries lithium sans anode plus pratiques pour une utilisation réelle. Ce nouveau design permet à la batterie de durer plus longtemps et de fonctionner en toute sécurité sans pièces supplémentaires, ce qui peut réduire les coûts et le poids. Cette amélioration pourrait conduire à de meilleures batteries pour téléphones, ordinateurs portables et voitures électriques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement travailler à rendre ce design de batterie plus simple et moins coûteux à produire. Ils pourraient aussi le tester dans différentes conditions et appareils pour confirmer sa durabilité et sa sécurité avant une utilisation large.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle conception de batterie utilisant une couche d'électrolyte spéciale permet des batteries lithium sans anode à haute énergie et longue durée, pouvant améliorer les futurs appareils électroniques et véhicules électriques.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo diseño de batería de litio mejora energía y duración sin ánodo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las baterías de litio metal son importantes para almacenar energía en dispositivos y autos eléctricos. Un tipo especial llamado baterías de litio metal sin ánodo (AFLMB) puede almacenar más energía y ser más barato porque no usa material de ánodo. Pero hasta ahora, estas baterías tenían poca duración porque el litio no se deposita de manera uniforme, causando daños.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos desarrollaron una nueva batería que soluciona este problema usando un electrolito especial. Este electrolito crea una capa delgada y flexible en la superficie de la batería que ayuda al litio a distribuirse uniformemente y protege la batería durante la carga y descarga. Probaron una batería con este diseño que puede almacenar mucha energía y funcionó bien durante cientos de ciclos.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque hace que las baterías sin ánodo sean más prácticas para usar. El nuevo diseño ayuda a que la batería dure más y funcione de forma segura sin partes extras, lo que puede reducir costos y peso. Esta mejora podría llevar a mejores baterías para teléfonos, laptops y autos eléctricos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente trabajarán en hacer este diseño más fácil y barato de producir. También podrían probarlo en diferentes condiciones y dispositivos para asegurar su durabilidad y seguridad antes de usarlo ampliamente.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo diseño de batería con una capa especial de electrolito permite baterías de litio sin ánodo con alta energía y larga duración que podrían mejorar futuros dispositivos y autos eléctricos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a delicate, glowing mesh-like film gently enveloping and connecting a smooth, flat lithium metal surface, symbolizing uniform ion flow and stability, with soft, warm hues of pale gold and muted blues highlighting the seamless, self-adaptive structure within an abstract battery cell environment.

articles.title: Particle discovered at CERN solves a 20-year-old mystery
articles.summary: Physicists working on the LHCb experiment have spotted an elusive and fleeting particle, a heavier and more charming cousin to the proton, that has been sought for decades
articles.content:
Physicists working on the LHCb experiment have spotted an elusive and fleeting particle, a heavier and more charming cousin to the proton, that has been sought for decades

The LHCb experiment cavern at CERN CERN/Brice, Maximilien

A new particle has popped into existence at CERN’s Large Hadron Collider, a heavier proton-like particle that contains two charm quarks.

Protons and neutrons are examples of a class of particles called baryons, which each contain three fundamental subatomic particles called quarks that come in a variety of so-called flavours. In the case of a proton, there are two “up” quarks and one “down” quark that make up the particle.

Advertisement

But heavier quarks, like those known as charm quarks, can also combine to make baryons. However, because these unusual quark combinations are heavier and so more unstable, they often have fleetingly short lifetimes and quickly decay into other particles.

In 2017, physicists working at CERN’s LHCb experiment glimpsed one of these exotic baryons, memorably named Xi cc ++, that was made up of two charm quarks and an up quark. This particle lived for only a trillionth of a second. Now, physicists working on the LHCb experiment have spotted the charm-filled sister particle to Xi cc ++, called the Xi cc +particle, which contains a down quark instead of an up, making it a heavier analogue of the proton.

This particle had a predicted lifetime of six times shorter than that of the Xi cc ++, making it much harder to detect. It was found only after the LHCb experiment was upgraded to carry out more sensitive particle searches. The finding has a statistical significance of over 7 sigma, a measure that physicists use to state how confident they are that the result isn’t a random fluke, which easily clears the 5-sigma bar required to claim a discovery.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

“Not only is it interesting discovering the particle in its own right – the Xi cc + has been searched for for a long time – but it also really shows the power that these upgrades to the LHC are having,” says Chris Parkes at the University of Manchester in the UK. “In one year’s data sample, we were able to see something that we couldn’t see with 10 years of data from the previous generation.”

Spotting this particle could teach us about how the strong nuclear force, which describes how quarks bind together, glues together heavier quarks than those we see in protons and neutrons, says Parkes. But it also resolves a 20-year-old mystery.

In 2002, physicists working on the SELEX experiment at the Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory in Illinois thought that they had spotted a particle that looked very much like Xi cc +, but with a much lower mass than predicted at only a 4.7 sigma level of confidence. “Now we’ve found it, but it’s at a mass which is similar to its partner [Xi cc ++] that we found a few years ago, and not at the mass that was predicted by SELEX,” says Parkes. The strength of the new discovery closes the door on the question of this particle’s mass.

“It’s a very interesting measurement, but it’s unclear what we learn from it,” says Juan Rojo at Vrije University Amsterdam in the Netherlands. “There is no rule in quantum chromodynamics which prevents this hadron from existing, but now we’ve measured it exists, we are left not particularly illuminated.”

Part of this, says Rojo, is because our current theories don’t predict well how heavier quarks inside baryons should interact or what their masses should be. “The data is now ahead of the theory for these kinds of particles, but it could be that in five years from now, this measurement is able to answer some very important theory questions,” says Rojo, such as what different combinations of quarks mean for particle masses.
articles.reasoning: The discovery of a new particle at CERN's LHCb experiment solves a 20-year-old mystery and advances understanding of fundamental physics, representing a significant scientific breakthrough with broad implications for particle physics theory.
articles.personality_title: CERN scientists discover new particle that solves a 20-year mystery
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – At CERN’s Large Hadron Collider (LHC), scientists study tiny particles called quarks, which combine to form bigger particles like protons and neutrons. Some quarks, called charm quarks, are heavier and harder to observe because they quickly change into other particles.

**What happened** – Researchers working on the LHCb experiment at CERN found a new particle named Xi cc +. This particle is made of two charm quarks and one down quark, making it similar but heavier than a proton. It was detected after upgrades to the LHCb detector improved its ability to spot very short-lived particles. The discovery is very certain, with a confidence level called 7 sigma, which means it is very unlikely to be a mistake.

**Impact** – This discovery solves a question that has lasted 20 years. In 2002, a different experiment thought they had found this particle but with a different mass. The new finding shows the particle’s mass is close to its sibling particle, Xi cc ++, clearing up confusion. It also helps scientists learn more about how the strong force holds quarks together inside particles, especially when heavier quarks are involved.

**What's next step** – Scientists will use this discovery to improve their theories about how quarks combine and behave. Current theories don’t fully explain the masses and interactions of particles with heavy quarks, so this data will guide future research and might answer important questions in particle physics within the next few years.

**One-sentence takeaway** – CERN’s LHCb experiment discovered a new heavy particle, confirming its existence and clearing up a two-decade-old puzzle about its mass and behavior.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques du CERN découvrent une nouvelle particule qui résout un mystère de 20 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Au Grand collisionneur de hadrons (LHC) du CERN, les scientifiques étudient de toutes petites particules appelées quarks, qui s’assemblent pour former des particules plus grosses comme les protons et les neutrons. Certains quarks, appelés quarks charme, sont plus lourds et plus difficiles à observer car ils se transforment rapidement en d’autres particules.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les chercheurs travaillant sur l’expérience LHCb au CERN ont découvert une nouvelle particule nommée Xi cc +. Cette particule est composée de deux quarks charme et d’un quark down, ce qui la rend similaire mais plus lourde qu’un proton. Elle a été détectée après des améliorations du détecteur LHCb qui ont permis de voir des particules très instables. La découverte est très sûre, avec un niveau de confiance appelé 7 sigma, ce qui signifie qu’il est très peu probable qu’il s’agisse d’une erreur.

**Impact** – Cette découverte résout une question qui dure depuis 20 ans. En 2002, une autre expérience croyait avoir trouvé cette particule mais avec une masse différente. La nouvelle découverte montre que la masse de la particule est proche de celle de sa sœur, Xi cc ++, ce qui met fin à la confusion. Elle aide aussi les scientifiques à mieux comprendre comment la force forte maintient les quarks ensemble à l’intérieur des particules, surtout quand il s’agit de quarks plus lourds.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques utiliseront cette découverte pour améliorer leurs théories sur la façon dont les quarks s’assemblent et se comportent. Les théories actuelles n’expliquent pas complètement les masses et interactions des particules avec des quarks lourds, donc ces données guideront les recherches futures et pourraient répondre à des questions importantes en physique des particules dans les prochaines années.

**En une phrase** – L’expérience LHCb du CERN a découvert une nouvelle particule lourde, confirmant son existence et résolvant un mystère vieux de vingt ans sur sa masse et son comportement.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos del CERN descubren una nueva partícula que resuelve un misterio de 20 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En el Gran Colisionador de Hadrones (LHC) del CERN, los científicos estudian partículas muy pequeñas llamadas quarks, que se juntan para formar partículas más grandes como protones y neutrones. Algunos quarks, llamados quarks encanto, son más pesados y difíciles de observar porque cambian rápidamente a otras partículas.

**Qué pasó** – Los investigadores del experimento LHCb en el CERN encontraron una nueva partícula llamada Xi cc +. Esta partícula está formada por dos quarks encanto y un quark down, lo que la hace similar pero más pesada que un protón. Fue detectada después de que el detector LHCb fuera mejorado para poder ver partículas que duran muy poco. El descubrimiento es muy seguro, con un nivel de confianza llamado 7 sigma, que significa que es muy poco probable que sea un error.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento resuelve una pregunta que duraba 20 años. En 2002, otro experimento creyó haber encontrado esta partícula pero con una masa diferente. El nuevo hallazgo muestra que la masa de la partícula es cercana a la de su hermana, Xi cc ++, aclarando la confusión. También ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor cómo la fuerza fuerte mantiene unidos a los quarks dentro de las partículas, especialmente cuando se trata de quarks más pesados.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán este descubrimiento para mejorar sus teorías sobre cómo se combinan y se comportan los quarks. Las teorías actuales no explican completamente las masas e interacciones de partículas con quarks pesados, por lo que estos datos guiarán futuras investigaciones y podrían responder preguntas importantes en la física de partículas en los próximos años.

**Una frase clave** – El experimento LHCb del CERN descubrió una nueva partícula pesada, confirmando su existencia y resolviendo un misterio de dos décadas sobre su masa y comportamiento.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing particle cluster symbolizing two intertwined charm quarks and one down quark, radiating light within a softly illuminated, abstract cavern inspired by the LHCb experiment’s underground setting, using gentle natural tones of deep blues, warm golds, and subtle reds to evoke discovery and scientific wonder.
